context.description: A philosophical prose passage on consciousness compares dreaming, waking consciousness, and a possible higher state of awareness. The section "Der Drang nach oben" argues that each expansion of awareness reveals new rules, reshapes reality models, and changes judgment. It also uses the teacher-child relationship as an example of how maturity affects evaluation.
normalization_notes: Translated section heading from German to English.
Used document-level title from the source file name.

rows.paragraph_text: The text reflects on whether a learner or child can properly judge teaching or upbringing only after reaching the same level as the teacher or parent, and notes that both learners and teachers may lack maturity.
rows.text_content: The text reflects on whether a learner or child can properly judge teaching or upbringing only after reaching the same level as the teacher or parent, and notes that both learners and teachers may lack maturity.

rows.paragraph_text: It extends the analogy from dream consciousness to waking consciousness and speculates that a still higher consciousness would reveal new connections, rules, and a broader reality model that would change decisions.
rows.text_content: It extends the analogy from dream consciousness to waking consciousness and speculates that a still higher consciousness would reveal new connections, rules, and a broader reality model that would change decisions.

rows.paragraph_text: The argument concludes that such a model should allow predictions and internal contradictions in waking consciousness to be tested from above.
rows.text_content: The argument concludes that such a model should allow predictions and internal contradictions in waking consciousness to be tested from above.

content.free_text: book_page nonfiction_philosophy philosophy_of_consciousness Book: Bewusstseinsreisen - Version 2. Page 59. Section: Der Drang nach oben. Consciousness essay on dream consciousness, waking consciousness, higher awareness, and changing reality models.
